romania.title: CNIR a aprobat proiectul pentru autorizația de construire a lotului Joseni-Ditrău, primul tronson montan al A8
romania.summary: Compania Națională de Investiții Rutiere (CNIR) a aprobat proiectul pentru autorizația de construire (PAC) pentru lotul Joseni - Ditrău, primul lot al secțiunii montane a Autostrăzii Unirii A8, înainte de termenul prevăzut în contract, a anunțat secretarul de stat în Ministerul Transporturilor și Infrastructurii, Horațiu Cosma. Potrivit acestuia, este și primul lot de autostradă construit vreodată în județul Harghita.

Potrivit oficialului, investițiile sunt menite să ofere o alternativă modernă și nepoluantă la traficul rutier din jurul celor două mari centre urbane.

romania.title: Avertisment de la IGSU: Nu lăsați copiii și animalele singuri în mașină. Le puneți viața în pericol
romania.summary: Pompierii îi avertizează pe părinți despre unul dintre riscurile ignorate în perioada verii. Mașinile se încing foarte tare, chiar și atunci când temperaturile nu sunt mari, și devin periculoase pentru copii și pentru animale, dacă rămân blocați în interior.

Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... medical.title: Immunotherapy may temporarily restore fertility in patients of premature menopause
medical.summary: A pilot study from Karolinska Institutet shows that immunotherapy may enable the stimulation of egg maturation in women with autoimmune POI (premature ovarian insufficiency)—a condition that usually leads to infertility. Three out of 10 women gave birth to healthy babies following the treatment.

medical.title: Seeing through optical noise: New method offers sharper way to image the eye
medical.summary: Optical coherence tomography (OCT) has become one of the foundations of modern ophthalmology. A patient sits in front of the device and focuses on a target, and moments later the physician can see a detailed cross-section of the retina, layer by layer, without any physical contact with the eye. It is one of the most significant advances in ophthalmology over the past three decades. OCT enables clinicians to detect glaucoma, age-related macular degeneration, diabetic retinopathy and macular edema before a patient experiences noticeable vision loss.

medical.title: The universe may be hiding conscious minds stranger than we can imagine
medical.summary: What if consciousness isn’t limited to brains like ours? Philosophers Eric Schwitzgebel and Jeremy Pober argue that consciousness could arise in many different forms of life, even in beings built from radically different materials than those found on Earth. Drawing on the vastness of the universe and the likely existence of countless alien civilizations, they suggest it would be surprisingly Earth-centric to assume that only Earth-like biology can support conscious experience.

medical.title: Tourist hotspots could be creating new routes for parasite transmission
medical.summary: Researchers from the University of Lincoln have contributed to an international study examining how interactions between people, free-roaming dogs and wildlife at urban tourist destinations may create opportunities for parasite transmission.

medical.title: Scientists discover ancient brain cells that help block distractions
medical.summary: Scientists have discovered a tiny group of neurons in an ancient brain region that acts like a built-in focus filter, helping the brain ignore distractions and zero in on what matters most. When researchers temporarily switched off these neurons in mice, the animals became unusually distractible—similar to what is seen in ADHD—but regained normal focus as soon as the neurons were reactivated.

medical.title: Six-month results confirm feasibility and early success of first in-human combined bladder-kidney transplant
medical.summary: The first-in-human fully vascularized bladder graft transplanted with a kidney has reached a promising six-month milestone, according to a new feasibility study published in The Lancet. The procedure, performed in May 2025, demonstrated technical feasibility and early functional success at six months after surgery.

medical.title: Dementia could cost US $818 billion in 2026, with unpaid care driving burden
medical.summary: A new USC-led study finds Alzheimer's disease and related dementias will cost the United States an estimated $818 billion this year, driven largely by often-overlooked costs to persons living with dementia and family and friends providing their care.

medical.title: Think you're eating healthy? You may be missing this heart-protecting nutrient
medical.summary: Eating five servings of fruits and vegetables may not be enough if you're missing foods rich in flavanols, a group of compounds linked to better heart health. Researchers found that choices like blackberries, plums, apples, broad beans, cherries, and green tea can dramatically increase flavanol intake.

medical.title: Scientists reprogram brain immune cells to fight Alzheimer’s
medical.summary: A newly identified molecule called OLE helped restore the brain’s immune cells to a more protective state in Alzheimer’s models. The treatment reduced toxic plaque buildup and improved memory, raising hopes for a new therapeutic approach.

science.title: Artificial DNA tiles could deliver drugs and monitor neurons non-disruptively
science.summary: Living cells constantly exchange ions (i.e., charged particles) via the thin barrier that surrounds their interior, known as the outer membrane. Neuroscientists and medical researchers have long been trying to devise effective methods to measure this exchange of ions, which is known to be associated with communication between neurons and various other crucial physiological processes.

science.title: New giant wormlion fly species identified on the southern slopes of the Himalayas
science.summary: An enigmatic new species of wormlion fly, whose larvae construct clever pitfall traps to capture prey, has been revealed in a study led by researchers at Dali University in China.

science.title: More people today have a stronger belief in their own ability to shape their lives
science.summary: People living in Germany have more confidence in themselves today than 20 years ago. They have more faith in their ability to influence their own lives and key life events. This has now been shown in a long-term study conducted by Dr. Theresa M. Entringer from the University of Greifswald and colleagues from Berlin and the U.S., based on the Socio-Economic Panel (SOEP). Data from more than 42,000 individuals were analyzed for this purpose. The examination period spanned more than two decades; those interviewed were ages 16 to 98. The study was published in the European Journal of Personality.

science.title: Why female guppies prefer rare males and how this might shape evolution
science.summary: When it comes to choosing a partner, some species prefer males that stand out from the crowd. Evolutionary biologists call the resulting process negative frequency-dependent selection. It means that a male has a huge mating advantage when his appearance is rare but loses that advantage if his look becomes too common. This constant balancing act may be one important reason for the variety in nature.

science.title: Asteroid zooming past Earth on Saturday visible to stargazers
science.summary: A large asteroid that will zoom harmlessly past Earth on Saturday will be visible to stargazers using a small telescope or large binoculars, the European Space Agency announced Wednesday.

science.title: By making key signaling molecules called β-arrestins into druggable targets, scientists crack long-standing challenge
science.summary: To function normally, nearly every cell in the human body relies on G-protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) to receive and send signals. That's why GPCRs are targeted by roughly one-third of all FDA-approved drugs.

science.title: Turning low-value diamond dust into high-performance quantum materials
science.summary: Diamonds have long been coveted for their beauty. Their dazzling color and clarity make them perfect candidates for luxury jewelry. However, it's their other unique characteristics, including their hardness, thermal conductivity and chemical resistance, that make diamonds suitable for various applications in industry and advanced technologies.

science.title: Quantum squeezing sidesteps the limits on mechanical transducers
science.summary: From detecting the ripples of colliding black holes to imaging individual chemical bonds, mechanical transducers have repeatedly transformed our understanding of the universe. So far, however, the sensitivity of these devices has been intrinsically limited by the laws of quantum mechanics itself.

science.title: Axolotl-inspired skin matrix may help heal wounds with less scarring
science.summary: Researchers in Taiwan have developed a cell-free extracellular matrix material from axolotl skin that helped mouse burn wounds close faster and show signs of reduced fibrotic scarring. The findings suggest that one of nature's most remarkable regenerators may inspire future strategies for burns, chronic wounds and surgical injuries, but the approach is not yet a treatment for human wounds.

science.title: Scientists catch classical space-time crystals moving like Majorana quasiparticles
science.summary: A research team from Hiroshima University, the University of Colorado, and other collaborators have demonstrated that space-time crystals—exotic structures that, under external drive, loop endlessly through both space and time—can be created using everyday liquid-crystal materials.

science.title: Voluntary corporate climate goals are viewed favorably by investors, researchers discover
science.summary: As companies face increasing pressure to address climate change, many are choosing to publicly announce voluntary carbon-elimination goals.

science.title: Sugar-coated nanoparticles show promise for treating most aggressive form of brain cancer
science.summary: Researchers at Oregon State University have potentially found a new way to treat the most aggressive form of brain cancer, glioblastoma, whose two-year survival rate is less than 30%.

science.title: Five years of aerosol remote sensing in Mindelo—a milestone in atmospheric research in the Atlantic
science.summary: For five years now, a distinctive green laser beam has been shining at night up to 30 kilometers (19 miles) above the harbor of the island's capital. It forms part of a high-energy lidar with which the Leibniz Institute for Atmospheric Research (TROPOS) operates continuous aerosol and cloud measurements at the Ocean Science Center Mindelo (OSCM).

science.title: From virtue to vice: How the morality of popular music lyrics has changed since the 1960s
science.summary: Popular music may be reflecting a growing culture of vices, according to new research from the Center for Digital Music at Queen Mary University of London. The analysis of musical evolution found that song lyrics have become increasingly negative over the past six decades, with declining references to moral virtues. This could provide an important indicator of a cultural shift in society.

science.title: Hot Jupiter endures star-powered barbecue
science.summary: You're the grillmaster at the annual family Fourth of July barbecue, and you're sweating bullets standing over the grill in the sweltering summer heat. You're trying to stay cool by pressing a cold beer can to your forehead, but to no avail. You can't go inside because, once again, you're the grillmaster and need to watch the food simmering on your freshly cleaned grill. Your brother-in-law is a university astronomy professor and walks over, asking how you're doing. You say, "This heat is killing me. I feel hotter than the barbecue!" Your science teacher brother-in-law slyly says, "Try being an exoplanet." You roll your eyes.

science.title: Do hyenas eat livestock and rhinos? Behavioral biologists investigate a surprisingly charming population
science.summary: For conservation and the management of human–wildlife conflicts, it is of great interest to know which species are eaten by carnivores. Scientists from the Ngorongoro Hyena Project at the Leibniz Institute for Zoo and Wildlife Research (Leibniz-IZW) analyzed this dynamic for spotted hyenas in the Ngorongoro Crater in Tanzania using DNA analysis of hundreds of fecal samples collected over 24 years.

science.title: Scientists expected a black hole but found a neutrino factory powered by stars
science.summary: A distant galaxy nicknamed Shadow Blaster may have revealed a surprising source of cosmic neutrinos: extreme star formation instead of a supermassive black hole. The discovery suggests that hidden, dust-filled starburst galaxies could account for a significant fraction of the Universe’s high-energy neutrinos.

science.title: Researchers found a Wordle strategy that wins 99% of the time
science.summary: Researchers developed a Wordle-solving strategy that succeeds 99% of the time by focusing on information gain rather than likely answers. The method uses Shannon entropy to identify guesses that reveal the most about the hidden word. Each guess is designed to slash uncertainty and narrow the possibilities faster. The result significantly outperformed more traditional Wordle tactics.

science.title: Einstein’s “biggest blunder” may finally have an explanation
science.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising connection between quantum gravity and an exotic quantum state of matter that could explain why the universe isn’t expanding wildly fast. The study suggests that the very shape of space-time may protect the cosmological constant from disruptive quantum effects.

science.title: DNA time stamps reveal the strawberry’s surprising origins
science.summary: Researchers have created a new way to reconstruct the evolutionary history of complex plant genomes by analyzing genetic traces left by transposable elements. The technique revealed that modern strawberries were assembled through multiple ancient genome-merging events, shedding new light on how major crop species evolved.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

mix_items.title: Trupa byron continuă turneul simfonic „Minunata Lume Nouă” cu un concert alături de Orchestra Simfonică a Filarmonicii „Paul Constantinescu” din Ploiești
mix_items.summary: După succesul producției „Minunata Lume Nouă” prezentate la Sala Palatului și după concertele susținute în mai multe orașe din țară, trupa byron continuă turneul simfonic pe 25 iunie 2026, la Casa de… Articolul Trupa byron continuă turneul simfonic „Minunata Lume Nouă” cu un concert alături de Orchestra Simfonică a Filarmonicii „Paul Constantinescu” din Ploiești apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: JAZZx 2026 începe săptămâna viitoare – concerte Free Nationals, Samara Joy, Asaf Avidan și noul val al jazzului internațional. Cinci zile de muzică, patru scene și acces gratuit în inima Timișoarei
mix_items.summary: Între 1 și 5 iulie, Timișoara devine din nou capitala jazzului contemporan și a muzicii fără granițe. Ajuns la ediția cu numărul 14, JAZZx transformă orașul într-un spațiu deschis al… Articolul JAZZx 2026 începe săptămâna viitoare – concerte Free Nationals, Samara Joy, Asaf Avidan și noul val al jazzului internațional. Cinci zile de muzică, patru scene și acces gratuit în inima Timișoarei apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: CNIR a aprobat proiectul pentru autorizația de construire a lotului Joseni-Ditrău, primul tronson montan al A8
mix_items.summary: Compania Națională de Investiții Rutiere (CNIR) a aprobat proiectul pentru autorizația de construire (PAC) pentru lotul Joseni - Ditrău, primul lot al secțiunii montane a Autostrăzii Unirii A8, înainte de termenul prevăzut în contract, a anunțat secretarul de stat în Ministerul Transporturilor și Infrastructurii, Horațiu Cosma. Potrivit acestuia, este și primul lot de autostradă construit vreodată în județul Harghita.

mix_items.summary: Ministerul Transporturilor a avizat, miercuri, proiectele de tren metropolitan pentru București și Iași, a anunțat secretarul de stat în Ministerul Transporturilor și Infrastructurii, Horațiu Cosma. Potrivit oficialului, investițiile sunt menite să ofere o alternativă modernă și nepoluantă la traficul rutier din jurul celor două mari centre urbane.

mix_items.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... mix_items.title: Artificial DNA tiles could deliver drugs and monitor neurons non-disruptively
mix_items.summary: Living cells constantly exchange ions (i.e., charged particles) via the thin barrier that surrounds their interior, known as the outer membrane. Neuroscientists and medical researchers have long been trying to devise effective methods to measure this exchange of ions, which is known to be associated with communication between neurons and various other crucial physiological processes.

mix_items.title: World Vision România investește în formarea viitorilor specialiști ai agriculturii românești. O seră didactică modernizată cu tehnologii de Agricultură 4.0, inaugurată la Liceul Agricol „Sandu Aldea” din Călărași.

mix_items.summary: Atelierele în comunitate organizate de Fundația Inocenți îi ajută pe copii să își dezvolte încrederea în sine, abilitățile emoționale și relațiile sociale. The post Ateliere în comunitate, sprijin pentru dezvoltarea copiilor appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: Immunotherapy may temporarily restore fertility in patients of premature menopause
mix_items.summary: A pilot study from Karolinska Institutet shows that immunotherapy may enable the stimulation of egg maturation in women with autoimmune POI (premature ovarian insufficiency)—a condition that usually leads to infertility. Three out of 10 women gave birth to healthy babies following the treatment.

mix_items.title: New giant wormlion fly species identified on the southern slopes of the Himalayas
mix_items.summary: An enigmatic new species of wormlion fly, whose larvae construct clever pitfall traps to capture prey, has been revealed in a study led by researchers at Dali University in China.

mix_items.summary: Fundația World Vision România solicită Camerei Deputaților respingerea proiectul de lege privind finanțarea ONG-urilor, adoptat de Senat (L233/2026). Inițiativa legislativă... mix_items.title: More people today have a stronger belief in their own ability to shape their lives
mix_items.summary: People living in Germany have more confidence in themselves today than 20 years ago. They have more faith in their ability to influence their own lives and key life events. This has now been shown in a long-term study conducted by Dr. Theresa M. Entringer from the University of Greifswald and colleagues from Berlin and the U.S., based on the Socio-Economic Panel (SOEP). Data from more than 42,000 individuals were analyzed for this purpose. The examination period spanned more than two decades; those interviewed were ages 16 to 98. The study was published in the European Journal of Personality.

mix_items.title: Gândirea civică – concertul unei generații: povestea lui Ionuț, un tânăr în fața provocărilor cu care se confruntă tinerii de astăzi
mix_items.summary: Ionuț, elev în clasa a XI-a la un liceu din Municipiul Calafat, pasionat de muzică, cu un glas folcloric aparte,... mix_items.summary: Tiana are 21 de ani și face voluntariat de aproximativ 3 ani în programele și activitățile World Vision România. A crescut... Articolul Când dorința de a face bine este mai puternică decât emoțiile: Tiana și dragostea ei pentru voluntariat apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Seeing through optical noise: New method offers sharper way to image the eye
mix_items.summary: Optical coherence tomography (OCT) has become one of the foundations of modern ophthalmology. A patient sits in front of the device and focuses on a target, and moments later the physician can see a detailed cross-section of the retina, layer by layer, without any physical contact with the eye. It is one of the most significant advances in ophthalmology over the past three decades. OCT enables clinicians to detect glaucoma, age-related macular degeneration, diabetic retinopathy and macular edema before a patient experiences noticeable vision loss.

mix_items.title: Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional
mix_items.summary: Robert are 18 ani și este reprezentant al copiilor în Consiliul Consultativ al Copiilor și Tinerilor din cadrul World Vision România. Prin... Articolul Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Why female guppies prefer rare males and how this might shape evolution
mix_items.summary: When it comes to choosing a partner, some species prefer males that stand out from the crowd. Evolutionary biologists call the resulting process negative frequency-dependent selection. It means that a male has a huge mating advantage when his appearance is rare but loses that advantage if his look becomes too common. This constant balancing act may be one important reason for the variety in nature.

mix_items.title: Asteroid zooming past Earth on Saturday visible to stargazers
mix_items.summary: A large asteroid that will zoom harmlessly past Earth on Saturday will be visible to stargazers using a small telescope or large binoculars, the European Space Agency announced Wednesday.

mix_items.title: By making key signaling molecules called β-arrestins into druggable targets, scientists crack long-standing challenge
mix_items.summary: To function normally, nearly every cell in the human body relies on G-protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) to receive and send signals. That's why GPCRs are targeted by roughly one-third of all FDA-approved drugs.

mix_items.title: Turning low-value diamond dust into high-performance quantum materials
mix_items.summary: Diamonds have long been coveted for their beauty. Their dazzling color and clarity make them perfect candidates for luxury jewelry. However, it's their other unique characteristics, including their hardness, thermal conductivity and chemical resistance, that make diamonds suitable for various applications in industry and advanced technologies.

mix_items.title: The universe may be hiding conscious minds stranger than we can imagine
mix_items.summary: What if consciousness isn’t limited to brains like ours? Philosophers Eric Schwitzgebel and Jeremy Pober argue that consciousness could arise in many different forms of life, even in beings built from radically different materials than those found on Earth. Drawing on the vastness of the universe and the likely existence of countless alien civilizations, they suggest it would be surprisingly Earth-centric to assume that only Earth-like biology can support conscious experience.

mix_items.title: Quantum squeezing sidesteps the limits on mechanical transducers
mix_items.summary: From detecting the ripples of colliding black holes to imaging individual chemical bonds, mechanical transducers have repeatedly transformed our understanding of the universe. So far, however, the sensitivity of these devices has been intrinsically limited by the laws of quantum mechanics itself.

mix_items.title: Tourist hotspots could be creating new routes for parasite transmission
mix_items.summary: Researchers from the University of Lincoln have contributed to an international study examining how interactions between people, free-roaming dogs and wildlife at urban tourist destinations may create opportunities for parasite transmission.

mix_items.title: Axolotl-inspired skin matrix may help heal wounds with less scarring
mix_items.summary: Researchers in Taiwan have developed a cell-free extracellular matrix material from axolotl skin that helped mouse burn wounds close faster and show signs of reduced fibrotic scarring. The findings suggest that one of nature's most remarkable regenerators may inspire future strategies for burns, chronic wounds and surgical injuries, but the approach is not yet a treatment for human wounds.

mix_items.title: Scientists catch classical space-time crystals moving like Majorana quasiparticles
mix_items.summary: A research team from Hiroshima University, the University of Colorado, and other collaborators have demonstrated that space-time crystals—exotic structures that, under external drive, loop endlessly through both space and time—can be created using everyday liquid-crystal materials.

mix_items.title: Voluntary corporate climate goals are viewed favorably by investors, researchers discover
mix_items.summary: As companies face increasing pressure to address climate change, many are choosing to publicly announce voluntary carbon-elimination goals.

mix_items.title: Sugar-coated nanoparticles show promise for treating most aggressive form of brain cancer
mix_items.summary: Researchers at Oregon State University have potentially found a new way to treat the most aggressive form of brain cancer, glioblastoma, whose two-year survival rate is less than 30%.

mix_items.title: Five years of aerosol remote sensing in Mindelo—a milestone in atmospheric research in the Atlantic
mix_items.summary: For five years now, a distinctive green laser beam has been shining at night up to 30 kilometers (19 miles) above the harbor of the island's capital. It forms part of a high-energy lidar with which the Leibniz Institute for Atmospheric Research (TROPOS) operates continuous aerosol and cloud measurements at the Ocean Science Center Mindelo (OSCM).

mix_items.title: From virtue to vice: How the morality of popular music lyrics has changed since the 1960s
mix_items.summary: Popular music may be reflecting a growing culture of vices, according to new research from the Center for Digital Music at Queen Mary University of London. The analysis of musical evolution found that song lyrics have become increasingly negative over the past six decades, with declining references to moral virtues. This could provide an important indicator of a cultural shift in society.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover ancient brain cells that help block distractions
mix_items.summary: Scientists have discovered a tiny group of neurons in an ancient brain region that acts like a built-in focus filter, helping the brain ignore distractions and zero in on what matters most. When researchers temporarily switched off these neurons in mice, the animals became unusually distractible—similar to what is seen in ADHD—but regained normal focus as soon as the neurons were reactivated.

mix_items.title: Six-month results confirm feasibility and early success of first in-human combined bladder-kidney transplant
mix_items.summary: The first-in-human fully vascularized bladder graft transplanted with a kidney has reached a promising six-month milestone, according to a new feasibility study published in The Lancet. The procedure, performed in May 2025, demonstrated technical feasibility and early functional success at six months after surgery.

mix_items.title: Hot Jupiter endures star-powered barbecue
mix_items.summary: You're the grillmaster at the annual family Fourth of July barbecue, and you're sweating bullets standing over the grill in the sweltering summer heat. You're trying to stay cool by pressing a cold beer can to your forehead, but to no avail. You can't go inside because, once again, you're the grillmaster and need to watch the food simmering on your freshly cleaned grill. Your brother-in-law is a university astronomy professor and walks over, asking how you're doing. You say, "This heat is killing me. I feel hotter than the barbecue!" Your science teacher brother-in-law slyly says, "Try being an exoplanet." You roll your eyes.

mix_items.title: Do hyenas eat livestock and rhinos? Behavioral biologists investigate a surprisingly charming population
mix_items.summary: For conservation and the management of human–wildlife conflicts, it is of great interest to know which species are eaten by carnivores. Scientists from the Ngorongoro Hyena Project at the Leibniz Institute for Zoo and Wildlife Research (Leibniz-IZW) analyzed this dynamic for spotted hyenas in the Ngorongoro Crater in Tanzania using DNA analysis of hundreds of fecal samples collected over 24 years.

mix_items.title: Dementia could cost US $818 billion in 2026, with unpaid care driving burden
mix_items.summary: A new USC-led study finds Alzheimer's disease and related dementias will cost the United States an estimated $818 billion this year, driven largely by often-overlooked costs to persons living with dementia and family and friends providing their care.

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: Think you're eating healthy? You may be missing this heart-protecting nutrient
mix_items.summary: Eating five servings of fruits and vegetables may not be enough if you're missing foods rich in flavanols, a group of compounds linked to better heart health. Researchers found that choices like blackberries, plums, apples, broad beans, cherries, and green tea can dramatically increase flavanol intake.

mix_items.title: Scientists expected a black hole but found a neutrino factory powered by stars
mix_items.summary: A distant galaxy nicknamed Shadow Blaster may have revealed a surprising source of cosmic neutrinos: extreme star formation instead of a supermassive black hole. The discovery suggests that hidden, dust-filled starburst galaxies could account for a significant fraction of the Universe’s high-energy neutrinos.

mix_items.title: Researchers found a Wordle strategy that wins 99% of the time
mix_items.summary: Researchers developed a Wordle-solving strategy that succeeds 99% of the time by focusing on information gain rather than likely answers. The method uses Shannon entropy to identify guesses that reveal the most about the hidden word. Each guess is designed to slash uncertainty and narrow the possibilities faster. The result significantly outperformed more traditional Wordle tactics.

mix_items.title: Scientists reprogram brain immune cells to fight Alzheimer’s
mix_items.summary: A newly identified molecule called OLE helped restore the brain’s immune cells to a more protective state in Alzheimer’s models. The treatment reduced toxic plaque buildup and improved memory, raising hopes for a new therapeutic approach.

mix_items.title: Einstein’s “biggest blunder” may finally have an explanation
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising connection between quantum gravity and an exotic quantum state of matter that could explain why the universe isn’t expanding wildly fast. The study suggests that the very shape of space-time may protect the cosmological constant from disruptive quantum effects.

mix_items.title: DNA time stamps reveal the strawberry’s surprising origins
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a new way to reconstruct the evolutionary history of complex plant genomes by analyzing genetic traces left by transposable elements. The technique revealed that modern strawberries were assembled through multiple ancient genome-merging events, shedding new light on how major crop species evolved.

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Planul pe weekend e simplu: sa ma odihnesc de cat m-am gandit la plan.

satire_ro.title: Pesediștii insistă pentru Grindeanu premier! „Stăm cu cutiile de pantofi goale, vrem la guvernare!”
